Introduction
Modern transportation, communication, and logistics systems are often influenced by a decision
maker who can modify network parameters, e.g., tolls, capacities, or incentives, to steer how users
place demand. We model this as a Stackelberg (leader‚Äďfollower) interaction: the decision maker (the
leader) moves first, and a large population of selfish users (the followers) subsequently responds
[Migdalas, 1995, Korilis et al., 1997].
In many applications, followers choose discrete objects such as paths, trees, or schedules rather
than smooth flow splits. This yields combinatorial congestion games (CCGs), where the feasible
strategy set can be exponentially large and the equilibrium can change abruptly as the leader
perturbs parameters [Wardrop, 1952, Roughgarden and Tardos, 2002, Karakostas and Kolliopoulos,
2009]. See Appendix C for real-world examples of Stackelberg control in CCGs.

incentives) that affect follower costs. Followers are modeled as a nonatomic population of total
demand 1 that selects combinatorial strategies S from a family S built from n resources (e.g., paths

resource i. We write C for the set of feasible load vectors induced by distributions over strategies

Main challenge: the hyper-objective can be nonsmooth.
Even when the primitives (őł, y) 7‚Üí

is that C is a polytope: as őł varies, the optimizer y‚čÜ(őł) moves along faces, and small parameter
changes can alter which constraints are active, equivalently, which combinatorial strategies are used
with positive mass. These active-set switches create kinks in the equilibrium map and hence in ő¶
(see Example 2.7). As a consequence, differentiating through equilibria is delicate in CCGs, and the
leader‚Äôs problem cannot be treated as a standard smooth bilevel program.
Prior work: differentiable equilibrium computation.
A recent line of work makes the
equilibrium computation differentiable and then updates the leader parameters őł by backpropagating
through the lower-level iterations [Sakaue and Nakamura, 2021]. Concretely, they run an iterative
equilibrium solver for a fixed number of steps and differentiate through this computation to obtain
a hyper-gradient that optimizes the resulting surrogate objective evaluated at the approximate
equilibrium. However, convergence guarantees for optimizing this surrogate do not necessarily
imply convergence guarantees for the true hyper-objective ő¶(őł) = F(őł, y‚čÜ(őł)), especially when ő¶
is nonsmooth due to active-set changes, as can happen in CCGs. To turn surrogate guarantees
into guarantees for ő¶, one must control both the equilibrium approximation error and the bias
introduced by the differentiable approximation, as noted in Sakaue and Nakamura [2021]. A more
thorough discussion of related work appears in Appendix B.
Our approach: oracle-based optimization of the true (nonsmooth) objective.
We take
an alternative viewpoint: rather than enforcing differentiability of y‚čÜ(őł), we treat equilibrium
computation as a black box and optimize ő¶ without differentiating through the lower-level solver.
Our method has two coupled components: (i) an inner Frank‚ÄďWolfe routine approximates y‚čÜ(őł)
over C using only gradients ‚ąáyf(őł, ¬∑) and an exact or subsampled linear minimization oracle (LMO)
over C; and (ii) an outer zeroth-order loop that updates őł using two-point function evaluations of
bő¶T (őł) := F(őł, yT (őł)), where yT (őł) is the inner iterate after T steps.
Our approach has both practical and theoretical advantages. Practically, the inner Frank‚Äď
Wolfe loop never enumerates the (potentially huge) strategy set S: each step only needs a linear
minimization oracle (LMO) that, given weights g ‚ąąRn, returns a minimum-cost strategy in

In terms of theoretical guarantees, first, for subsampled Frank‚ÄďWolfe, we isolate a single optimizer-
hit parameter őļm, the probability that the sampled candidate set contains an exact LMO minimizer,
and prove an O(1/(őļmT)) convergence rate. This recovers the standard subsampled Frank‚ÄďWolfe
guarantee under uniform-inclusion sampling, i.e., each strategy has the same probability of appearing
in the candidate set (for example, by sampling m strategies uniformly at random) [Kerdreux et al.,
2018, Theorem 2.1], while allowing general (non-uniform, structure-aware) sampling distributions
quantified solely by őļm. Second, we prove that the full bilevel procedure converges to a generalized
Goldstein stationary point of the hyper-objective ő¶ [Goldstein, 1977], with an explicit dependence
on the inner equilibrium approximation error.
Contributions.
Our main contributions are as follows.
‚ÄĘ Since the hyper-objective ő¶(őł) = F(őł, y‚čÜ(őł)) is typically nonsmooth in CCGs, assuming it is
differentiable is unrealistic.
We therefore propose ZO-Stackelberg, an oracle-based bilevel
algorithm that targets the original ő¶ without differentiating through equilibria by coupling an inner
Frank‚ÄďWolfe equilibrium solver with an outer zeroth-order update on őł.
‚ÄĘ We establish an end-to-end convergence guarantee for ZO-Stackelberg: the method converges
to generalized Goldstein stationary points (GGSPs) of the (Lipschitz, possibly nonsmooth) hyper-
objective ő¶, with explicit dependence on the inner equilibrium approximation error.
‚ÄĘ To make the inner equilibrium solver scalable when exact LMOs are expensive, we use and analyze
subsampled Frank‚ÄďWolfe and prove an O(1/(őļmT)) rate under a mild optimizer-hit assumption,
where őļm is the probability that the sampled candidate set contains an exact LMO minimizer. We
also propose stratified (e.g., length-debiased) sampling schemes that keep őļm nontrivial in large
strategy spaces.
‚ÄĘ We provide an efficient Python implementation with both exact and subsampled LMOs (including
s‚Äďt path, Hamiltonian path, and Steiner cycle strategy families).
Experiments on real-world
transportation networks show that ZO-Stackelberg matches the accuracy of a differentiation-
based baseline while achieving orders-of-magnitude speedups with drastically reduced memory
usage.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 formalizes the Stackelberg model of
CCGs. Section 3 presents the bilevel algorithm. Section 4 discusses implementations of the LMO.
Section 5 provides the convergence analysis, and Section 6 reports empirical results.

4.2
Exact & Approximate LMO for NP-hard Families
For NP-hard families, the LMO is itself NP-hard as a standalone combinatorial optimization problem.
However, across FW iterations (and across outer iterations), the feasible family S remains fixed
and only the additive weights g vary. We leverage this structure by compiling S once into a
zero-suppressed binary decision diagram (ZDD) [ichi Minato, 1993]. A ZDD is a directed acyclic
graph (DAG) that compactly represents a family of subsets (i.e., S) by sharing identical subproblems,
often making it far smaller than the explicit strategy set. In our setting, each root-to-‚ä§path encodes
a feasible strategy, where ‚ä§denotes the accepting terminal (see Appendix G for notation and a
primer). Although compilation can be exponential in n in the worst case, it amortizes the cost
of NP-hardness and enables many subsequent LMO queries without re-solving the problem from
scratch.

Approximate LMO via Subsampling.
When the ZDD is too large for repeated exact minimiza-

best one(s) as a subsampled oracle LMOm. We discuss sampling distributions and analyze the

experiments, LMOm closely tracks the exact ZDD oracle while remaining practical when repeated

Unlike Sakaue and Nakamura [2021], who smooth the LMO to backpropagate through FW
iterates, we use ZDDs only as exact or sampled combinatorial oracles inside standard FW. The
outer loop then optimizes the true (typically nonsmooth) hyper-objective without differentiating
through equilibria.

exact minimization is feasible only via a ZDD oracle or must be approximated by sampling.

Related Work
Stackelberg control and congestion games.
Congestion games and selfish routing are classical
models for large-scale transportation and communication systems, with Wardrop equilibria as the
standard notion of flow-level equilibrium [Wardrop, 1952]. The potential formulation of Wardrop
equilibria and its connection to convex optimization dates back to the Beckmann transformation
[Beckmann et al., 1956] and has since been widely used in algorithmic and economic analyses of
routing [Roughgarden and Tardos, 2002, Sandholm, 2010]. Stackelberg control in these models
17
includes leader decisions such as tolling, pricing, or capacity design that influence equilibrium
behavior; see, e.g., Karakostas and Kolliopoulos [2009] and references therein.
More general
Stackelberg formulations in congestion games allow richer action sets (e.g., multiple user classes
and non-singleton actions), further motivating combinatorial strategy spaces beyond simple path
choices [Marchesi et al., 2019]. Related work also studies how monetary interventions can steer
the system between equilibria [Huang et al., 2024]. For large networks, equilibrium computation is
typically performed using methods that exploit shortest-path computations and other combinatorial
subroutines. Classic traffic-assignment algorithms include Frank‚ÄďWolfe-type schemes specialized to
the Beckmann potential [LeBlanc et al., 1975]. Since Wardrop equilibria coincide with minimizers
of the (convex) Beckmann potential, each Frank‚ÄďWolfe step solves the linearization of the potential
over the feasible flow polytope; in network models this reduces to an all-or-nothing assignment
subproblem (typically implemented via shortest-path computations). This yields a projection-free
method whose per-iteration cost is dominated by combinatorial primitives rather than Euclidean
projections, which is one reason Frank‚ÄďWolfe is widely used in practice. Comprehensive discussions
of step-size rules (e.g., exact line search vs. method-of-successive-averages heuristics), convergence,
and practical variants can be found in standard references such as Sheffi [1984], Patriksson [1994].
Compact representations of combinatorial strategy sets.
When the strategy family S is
exponentially large, one approach is to represent S implicitly via compact data structures that share
substructure across many feasible strategies. Binary decision diagrams (BDDs) encode a Boolean
function as a reduced, ordered decision DAG [Bryant, 1986]; in our setting, the function is simply
the indicator of feasibility over subsets of [n]. Zero-suppressed BDDs (ZDDs) modify the reduction
rules to better match families of sparse sets, roughly, instances where most sets omit most items,
which often leads to dramatically smaller diagrams in practice [ichi Minato, 1993]. Once constructed,
the ZDD supports efficient dynamic programming on the DAG to perform common set-family
operations (e.g., union/intersection), to count feasible strategies, to sample feasible strategies, and
to optimize additive costs over S without enumerating it [ichi Minato, 1993, Bergman et al., 2018].
Although ZDD size is problem- and ordering-dependent (and can be exponential in the worst
case), decision-diagram techniques have become a practical tool for discrete optimization and for
implementing combinatorial oracles over large strategy spaces [Bergman et al., 2018]. Conceptually,
while our use of ZDDs is similar to the equilibrium computations in Sakaue and Nakamura [2021],
our role for them is different. We use ZDDs purely as a combinatorial oracle for linear minimization
inside Frank‚ÄďWolfe; the inner loop remains the standard (non-smoothed) FW method, and we never
require its iterations to be differentiable with respect to őł. This stands in contrast to differentiable
approaches that modify the FW steps (e.g., via softmin relaxations) to enable backpropagation
through the equilibrium map and rely on gradient-based updates for the leader‚Äôs problem. Our
algorithm instead keeps the equilibrium computation as a black box and handles the non-smooth
dependence on őł entirely at the outer level via zeroth-order methods.
Differentiating through equilibria and decision diagrams.
A contrasting line of work
makes the equilibrium computation itself differentiable and then applies gradient-based bilevel
optimization by differentiating through the lower-level computation. For Stackelberg CCGs, Sakaue
and Nakamura [2021] leverage ZDDs to represent S and develop a differentiable Frank‚ÄďWolfe-style
equilibrium solver via a softmin relaxation of the LMO, enabling automatic differentiation of the
surrogate map őł 7‚ÜíyT (őł) and the corresponding surrogate objective value F(őł, yT (őł)). More broadly,
differentiable optimization layers enable backpropagation through optimization problems [Amos
and Kolter, 2017, Agrawal et al., 2019]. This yields a practical hyper-gradient pipeline and strong
18
empirical performance, but the optimized objective is a smoothed surrogate rather than the true
hyper-objective ő¶(őł) = F(őł, y‚čÜ(őł)). Establishing end-to-end guarantees for ő¶ requires quantifying
both the equilibrium approximation error yT (őł) ‚Čąy‚čÜ(őł) and the mismatch between the gradient
of surrogate (‚ąáF(őł, yT (őł))) and suitable (generalized) derivatives of ő¶; this is explicitly noted as
challenging in Sakaue and Nakamura [2021]. In contrast, our method avoids differentiating through
equilibria and provides convergence to generalized Goldstein stationary points of the (typically
Lipschitz but nonsmooth) hyper-objective, with an explicit dependence on the inner equilibrium
error.
Zeroth-order (derivative-free) optimization.
Because the hyper-objective ő¶(őł) = F(őł, y‚čÜ(őł))
is typically Lipschitz but nonsmooth due to active-set changes in y‚čÜ(őł), we adopt a weak first-
order stationarity notion based on Clarke and Goldstein subdifferentials [Clarke, 1990, Goldstein,
1977]. These generalized derivatives and stationarity measures also underpin classic algorithms for
nonsmooth nonconvex optimization, most notably gradient sampling [Burke et al., 2005], as well as
modern analyses of stochastic methods on tame/nonsmooth landscapes [Davis et al., 2019, Liu et al.,
2024]. Our outer loop is a zeroth-order method based on randomized smoothing and two-point finite-
difference estimators, a standard approach in derivative-free optimization and bandit/zeroth-order
learning: foundational results include Flaxman et al. [2005], with refined rates and smoothing-based
analyses developed in, e.g., Duchi et al. [2015], Nesterov and Spokoiny [2017], Ghadimi and Lan
[2013]. Zeroth-order methods have also been analyzed in game-theoretic settings, including convex‚Äď
concave minmax formulations and (strongly) monotone games [Maheshwari et al., 2022, Drusvyatskiy
et al., 2022]. More classical derivative-free schemes include stochastic approximation methods such
as SPSA [Spall, 1992] and general-purpose trust-region frameworks for derivative-free optimization
[Conn et al., 2009]. Zeroth-order approaches are also useful in bilevel problems when the outer
objective is evaluated through an inner solve and may be nonsmooth or otherwise unsuitable for
backpropagation; see, e.g., Chen et al. [2025], Masiha et al. [2025].
Randomized and approximate Frank‚ÄďWolfe oracles.
To reduce the cost of LMO calls when
S is very large, several works study conditional-gradient methods with randomized or approximate
oracles. In particular, Kerdreux et al. [2018] analyze a subsampling-oracle Frank‚ÄďWolfe method
under a uniform-inclusion model, obtaining an O(1/(ő∑T)) rate. Related variants of stochastic/online
Frank‚ÄďWolfe have been studied in different settings, including projection-free stochastic optimization
[Hazan and Luo, 2016, Mokhtari et al., 2020]. Our analysis isolates a single optimizer-hit quantity
őļm and allows non-uniform, structure-aware sampling distributions tailored to imbalanced strategy
spaces.

All experiments were run on an HPC cluster node with an AMD

Zero-Suppressed Decision Diagrams (ZDD)
Zero-suppressed decision diagrams (ZDDs) are a canonical data structure for representing a family
of subsets of a finite universe in a compact form. They can be viewed as a specialization of reduced
ordered binary decision diagrams (BDDs) [Bryant, 1986] tailored to sparse set systems [ichi Minato,
1993]. In our setting, the universe typically consists of graph resources (e.g., edges), and a feasible
strategy (path, tree, cycle) is encoded by its incidence vector, i.e., a subset of universe items.
G.1
Definition and Construction
ZDDs as compressed set representations.
Fix an ordered universe U = (e1, . . . , en) and a
family S ‚äÜ2U. A ZDD for S is a rooted directed acyclic graph with two terminals ‚ä§and ‚ä•. Each
internal node v is labeled by an item index ‚Ąď(v) ‚ąą{1, . . . , n} and has two outgoing arcs: a lo-arc
corresponding to excluding e‚Ąď(v) and a hi-arc corresponding to including e‚Ąď(v). A root-to-‚ä§path
encodes a unique subset S ‚äÜU given by the labels whose hi-arcs are taken; the family represented
by the diagram is exactly the collection of subsets obtained from all root-to-‚ä§paths. Figure 4
illustrates this correspondence on a toy network.
The key advantage of ZDDs is that they admit aggressive reductions. As in BDDs, isomorphic
subgraphs are merged so that identical subproblems are shared [Bryant, 1986]. In addition, the
zero-suppression rule removes any node whose hi-child is ‚ä•, redirecting the incoming arc(s) to its
lo-child [ichi Minato, 1993]. Intuitively, if including an item can never lead to a feasible set, then
that item is irrelevant for the family and should be skipped. After applying these reductions, the
resulting diagram is canonical for a fixed variable ordering of U.
Construction and size.
ZDDs can be built by incremental, constraint-driven enumeration
procedures that traverse items in the chosen order and maintain a compact representation of partial
feasibility. For graph families (paths, Steiner structures, cycles), such constructions often proceed
by a frontier-style dynamic program that tracks only local connectivity information at the boundary
between processed and unprocessed edges. The resulting preprocessing time is roughly linear in the
number of ZDD nodes produced.
The practical challenge is that ZDD size is highly instance dependent. In the worst case, the
number of nodes can grow exponentially with n (and it depends sensitively on the chosen ordering),
which makes ZDD-based approaches unsuitable as a universal replacement for combinatorial opti-
mization. However, when the underlying constraints have exploitable structure, ZDDs can be orders
of magnitude smaller than the explicit family S and enable exact computations over exponentially
large strategy sets [ichi Minato, 1993].
G.2

A ZDD enables exact minimization of any additive weight function over the underlying family.

G.3
Sampling Strategies from a ZDD
Beyond exact optimization, ZDDs support efficient sampling of feasible strategies without enu-
merating S. This is useful when exact optimization becomes costly (e.g., for very large diagrams)
and one wishes to form a subsampled candidate set. Sampling is again implemented by dynamic
programming followed by a randomized root-to-‚ä§traversal.
Uniform sampling over strategies.
Let N(v) denote the number of root-to-‚ä§completions
from a node v. These counts satisfy N(‚ä§) = 1, N(‚ä•) = 0, and
N(v) = N(lo(v)) + N(hi(v)).
After computing N(v) for all nodes bottom-up, a uniformly random strategy is obtained by starting
at the root and, at each node v, choosing the lo-arc with probability N(lo(v))/N(v) and the hi-arc
with probability N(hi(v))/N(v). Whenever the hi-arc is chosen, we include the corresponding item
e‚Ąď(v). This produces an exactly uniform draw from S.
Length-stratified sampling.
In many graph families, the distribution of strategy sizes |S| is
highly skewed, so uniform sampling over S can overwhelmingly produce long, dense strategies.
To control the sampled lengths, it is convenient to first choose a target length and then sample
uniformly conditional on that length.
Let Nr(v) denote the number of completions from v that select exactly r additional items. These
length-refined counts satisfy N0(‚ä§) = 1, Nr(‚ä§) = 0 for r ‚Č•1, Nr(‚ä•) = 0, and

Complexity and numerical stability.
Once the diagram is built, uniform-strategy sampling
requires only the node counts N(v) and then one root-to-terminal traversal per sample. Length-

which each sample is generated by selecting a length and performing a single traversal with the
appropriate conditional probabilities. In practice the counts can be astronomically large, so in our
Python implementation, we store them in log-scale and to form the branching probabilities via
log-sum-exp arithmetic.

Poly-time strategy families.
When the strategy family admits a polynomial-time combinatorial
algorithm, the LMO can be implemented without preprocessing. For example, for s‚Äďt paths and
additive edge weights, the LMO is a shortest-path problem, solvable in O(|E| log |V |) time using
Dijkstra‚Äôs algorithm, where |V | and |E| = n are the numbers of vertices and edges in the graph.
NP-hard families with exact ZDD minimization.
For NP-hard families, the ZDD provides
an implicit representation of S that enables exact minimization of additive costs by dynamic
programming on the ZDD DAG. Once the diagram is built, a min-cost oracle call runs in time
linear in the ZDD size, O(NZDD). The worst-case NZDD can be exponential in n, but in many
structured instances it is far smaller than |S|; our experiments illustrate this gap across three real
transportation networks.
NP-hard families with subsampling.
When NZDD is too large for repeated exact minimization,
we still build the ZDD once and use it as a sampler. Uniform-strategy sampling requires only node
counts, while length-stratified sampling additionally uses length-refined counts up to ‚Ąďmax. Each
FW step then draws m feasible strategies and returns the best among the samples with respect
to the current linearization. This reduces the per-iteration cost to O(m ‚Ąďmax) at the expense of
replacing the exact minimizer with a randomized approximation.

Results and Analysis
Figures 1 and 2 report social-cost convergence and, at the final iterate, runtime per outer iteration,
peak RSS, FW gap, and social cost. Since a small FW gap certifies approximate equilibrium,
social-cost comparisons are meaningful only at small gaps. Across all scenarios, ZOS attains low FW
gaps and low social cost while being ‚Čą20‚Äď1000√ó faster per outer iteration and using at most 1.7 GiB,
whereas Diff uses 10 GiB to 194 GiB. The gains come from treating equilibrium computation as a
black box (no backprop through T=3000 inner steps) and parallelizing independent equilibrium
solves and sampled-strategy evaluations.
Scenario 1: Polynomial-time regime.
Since ZOS treats FW as a black box, in Scenario 1 we
plug in the exact shortest-path oracle and achieve Diff-level FW gaps (‚Čą2 √ó 10‚ąí2) with comparable
social cost. This yields a ‚Čą23√ó speedup and reduces peak RSS from 10.2 GiB to 0.28 GiB.
Scenario 2: NP-hard regime with tractable ZDD.
Scenario 2 is NP-hard, so both methods
rely on a ZDD; however, the ZDD is small enough that exact LMO minimization remains inexpensive.
Exact ZOS‚ÄďZDD matches Diff‚Äôs FW gap (about 10‚ąí2) and achieves comparable social cost, while
being roughly 61√ó faster and using far less memory (0.37 GiB vs. 15.9 GiB peak RSS). This gap is
largely explained by Diff backpropagating through the ZDD across all T inner iterations, whereas
ZOS treats the equilibrium solver as a black box and never differentiates through it. The subsampling
variants7 attain similarly low social cost, but their FW gaps plateau around 100 and they offer little
speedup over exact ZOS‚ÄďZDD. Overall, when the ZDD is tractable (e.g., fewer than 105 nodes),
exact ZOS‚ÄďZDD is the recommended choice.
Scenario 3: NP-hard regime with massive ZDD.
This setting yields a massive ZDD, making
repeated exact minimization costly; subsampling is therefore essential. Uniform-strategy sampling
(US) essentially stalls in both social cost and FW gap because the probability of hitting an LMO
optimizer is vanishingly small (indeed, őļm ‚Čąm/|S|). In contrast, our stratified schemes (UL/HL)
exploit the structure induced by nonnegative weights: when g ‚Č•0, adding resources cannot decrease
7Since all Hamiltonian s‚Äďt paths have the same length, the three sampling schemes coincide in this scenario.
12
the linear cost, so LMO minimizers are typically concentrated in smaller-cardinality (short) strata.
By over-sampling these strata, UL/HL dramatically increase the effective optimizer hit-rate and,
once m is large enough, match exact ZOS‚ÄďZDD in social cost and FW gap (FW gap ‚Čą10‚ąí3
at m=1000). Exact ZOS‚ÄďZDD attains similar accuracy but remains expensive (about 37s per
outer iteration), whereas UL/HL at m=1000 are roughly 6‚Äď7√ó faster, with < 1 GiB additional
memory. Diff is impractical in this regime (194 GiB peak) and failed to complete more than five
outer iterations within 10 hours.
